explanation: The input requires that once the system enters fail-safe mode, it cannot leave fail-safe mode (i.e., change to any other mode) until a power cycle occurs. In the given set of atomic propositions, 'failSafeMode' indicates that the system is currently in fail-safe mode, and 'off' indicates the system is fully powered off (allowing a power cycle). Since there is no explicit 'mode' proposition, we use 'failSafeMode' being true/false as the system being in or out of fail-safe mode. The property needed is that once 'failSafeMode' becomes true, it must remain true (i.e., stay in fail-safe mode) until the system is powered off. Therefore, the proper LTL property is: G (failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode V off)) This says: globally, if the system is in fail-safe mode, it remains in fail-safe mode until the system is powered off. Only after power-off can it leave fail-safe mode (thus, not exit earlier to another mode). This captures the essence of the input. Previous properties might incorrectly allow exit without a power cycle or might use temporal operators incorrectly, but the above keeps fail-safe active until a power cycle occurs.

The input requirement states that once the system enters 'fail-safe mode' (represented by 'failSafeMode'), it cannot return to any other mode unless a power cycle occurs (i.e., the device is turned off and then on again). In the provided atomic propositions, there is an 'off' discrete state, which we use to represent the system being turned off. There is no explicit 'power on' proposition, but the system going from 'off' to 'not off' (i.e., returning to some active mode) can be inferred as being powered on. Therefore, the correct LTL specification must guarantee that whenever the system enters fail-safe mode at a certain time, it cannot return to any other mode (i.e., it remains in failSafeMode) unless there is an intervening occurrence of 'off'. In LTL, this is best expressed by requiring that after failSafeMode holds, it must continuously hold until 'off' occurs. This is captured as: G(failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off)). This ensures that after failSafeMode is entered, only turning the power off allows exiting failSafeMode. Previous potential LTL attempts might either not establish this 'until off' constraint, or may allow exit from failSafeMode without a power cycle, which does not satisfy the requirement.

The natural language states that once the system is in 'fail-safe mode', it cannot leave this mode for any other operating mode unless a power cycle occurs (the system is fully powered off and then restarted). Since there is an atomic proposition 'failSafeMode' indicating fail-safe mode is active and 'off' indicating the system is de-energized and truly off, we express this as: whenever the system enters fail-safe mode, globally from that point on, it must remain in fail-safe mode at least until the system is turned off. In LTL, that's: whenever 'failSafeMode' holds, it holds until 'off'. We capture this with the formula: G(failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off)). This ensures that after entering fail-safe mode, the only way to leave it is via the system turning off ('off' becoming true), matching the requirement that the controller cannot return from fail-safe mode to another mode without a power cycle. Previous properties that simply required 'failSafeMode' never to be followed by non-failSafeMode states would overly constrain (e.g., not allowing recovery after full power off), or would not require persistence up to a power cycle.

The natural language states that once the controller enters fail-safe mode, it cannot return to any other mode until a power cycle occurs (off then on). In terms of the atomic propositions provided, 'failSafeMode' being true means the system is in fail-safe mode, and 'off' being true means the system is completely de-energized. Since there is no proposition directly representing 'return to another mode', the property must ensure that if the system has entered fail-safe mode, it stays there until it has been powered down ('off') at least once. This is best captured in LTL as: 'if failSafeMode is entered, then failSafeMode holds until off becomes true.' This ensures that failSafeMode cannot be exited (i.e., another mode entered) without an intervening 'off'. Previous (incorrect) LTL encodings, such as 'G(failSafeMode -> G(failSafeMode))', would not allow recovery after power off, and 'G(failSafeMode -> X(failSafeMode))' only requires the next step, so neither would strictly satisfy the input requirements. The used LTL operator is 'U' (until): failSafeMode U off.

The natural language property states that once the controller enters fail-safe mode, it cannot return to any other mode unless there is a power cycle (the machine is turned off and then turned on). In the provided atomic propositions, 'failSafeMode' indicates entering fail-safe mode, and 'off' indicates the system is off. There is no atomic proposition to indicate turning on, but we can infer that transitioning from 'off' (system de-energized) to not 'off' after being off corresponds to the system being turned on after being off.

The LTL formula must ensure that, once 'failSafeMode' holds, the system will remain in fail-safe mode ('failSafeMode') at all times, unless the system goes 'off' (i.e., until 'off' holds); only after 'off' has occurred is the restriction lifted. This is naturally modeled using the 'release' operator: as long as 'off' has not occurred, 'failSafeMode' must hold since it was first entered. In other words: 'Once failSafeMode becomes true, failSafeMode must continue to hold until the system goes off.' After 'off', the system may transition to another mode on restart (turning on again is outside the scope of available atomic props).

Why previous properties (such as just G(failSafeMode -> G(failSafeMode))) may not capture this: G(failSafeMode -> G(failSafeMode)) would prohibit ever leaving failSafeMode, even after a power cycle (off). The 'release' formulation allows taking that restriction away after off (power down), aligning with the natural language requirement.

LTL property: 'Globally, if failSafeMode occurs, failSafeMode must continue to hold until off occurs.'

The requirement states that once the controller enters fail-safe mode, it must not exit this mode except via a power cycle (i.e., turning the machine off and then on again). Translating this, we need to express that from the point where 'failSafeMode' becomes true, the system cannot switch to any other mode until the system is turned off ('off' becomes true), and only afterward (after 'off' becomes true) could the system move out of fail-safe mode. Since we do not have individual atomic propositions for 'other modes,' we will interpret it as 'it must remain in failSafeMode continuously unless the system is turned off.' This can be captured by the release operator as:  G(failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode V off)), which says: globally, whenever failSafeMode is active, it must hold (i.e., remain true) at least until the machine is turned off. This ensures failSafeMode cannot be exited unless there has been a power-off event. Older LTLs might try using 'U' or 'F' but may not tightly restrict that failSafeMode must persist until that off event happens (the release operator is needed for this strictness).

The natural language property specifies that once the controller enters fail-safe mode ('failSafeMode' becomes active), it cannot return to any other operating mode until the machine is powered off and on again (i.e., a power cycle, as indicated by 'off' becoming true and then false). In terms of LTL and the given atomic propositions, the best way to express this is as follows: 'Once failSafeMode is true, it must remain true until an 'off' state is observed. After 'off' occurs, failSafeMode can be cleared.' In LTL, this can be formalized as: G(failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off)) . This expresses that whenever the system is in fail-safe mode, it stays in that state until the system is powered off (off is true). Only after 'off' occurs can the controller leave fail-safe mode. If prior attempts used only invariants like G(failSafeMode -> G(failSafeMode)), they would not allow for exiting fail-safe at all; this translation better matches the requirement since it allows exit from fail-safe only after 'off' has occurred.

The requirement states: "Fail-safe Mode: The controller cannot return from fail-safe mode to any other mode without a power cycle (turn off and then turn on the machine)." 

The atomic propositions provided are: 'failSafeMode' (fail-safe is active) and 'off' (system is powered off). The property should ensure that once failSafeMode becomes active, it remains true until the system has been turned off (off becomes true). Only after this power cycle can failSafeMode become false again.

The most direct LTL formula for this is: G(failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off))

This states: Globally, if failSafeMode holds at any point, then failSafeMode must keep holding until off occurs. After off, failSafeMode is allowed to be deactivated/released.

This captures the notion that there is no return from failSafeMode to any other mode (i.e., no transition where failSafeMode becomes false) unless there is an intervening off state (power cycle).

Alternative formulas that, for example, might use 'X' (next) or try to constrain transitions more tightly, could be overly restrictive or fail to allow proper exit after off, whereas the above property directly ensures the requirement is met.

The property to express is that once the system enters fail-safe mode (failSafeMode becomes true), it cannot return to any other mode unless there is a power cycle (the machine is turned off and then back on). The only available atomic proposition for a power cycle is 'off', which indicates the system is completely de-energized. To encode this behavior, we specify: from the first occurrence of failSafeMode, until 'off' becomes true, the system must remain in failSafeMode. In LTL, this is: 'failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off)', globally (G), so it applies whenever failSafeMode becomes active. This prevents leaving fail-safe mode until after a power cycle (off event). Previous LTLs that simply say 'G(failSafeMode -> failSafeMode)' don't allow leaving fail-safe after power cycle, so such properties would be incorrect.

The natural language requirement states that once the controller has entered fail-safe mode, it cannot leave fail-safe mode (i.e., cannot go to another mode) unless there is a power cycle (the machine is turned off and then back on). In terms of atomic propositions, 'failSafeMode' is true when in fail-safe mode, and 'off' is true when the system is off. The property must enforce that, once 'failSafeMode' becomes true, it remains true until 'off' becomes true (i.e., until the system is powered off). This is best captured by: G (failSafeMode -> (failSafeMode U off)), which means 'globally, whenever failSafeMode holds, then failSafeMode must continue to hold until off becomes true.' This prevents the system from leaving fail-safe mode (failSafeMode = false) unless 'off' (system is powered down) intervenes, which aligns with the natural language intent. Previous properties that might use just 'G (failSafeMode -> G failSafeMode)' would not allow ever leaving fail-safe mode, even after a power cycle, which is too restrictive. Our property allows leaving fail-safe mode, but only via a power cycle.
